chunk_summary: Management of CMPA in infants emphasizes breastfeeding with maternal dietary adjustments or severity-based formula selection, avoiding unsafe substitutes like unmodified animal milk.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of breastfeeding and maternal dietary elimination in managing CMPA.
Identify appropriate formula options based on CMPA severity and response.
Recognize unsafe feeding practices and their risks in CMPA management.
key_facts: Breastfeeding is the first-line approach for CMPA management when feasible.
Maternal elimination diets require calcium and vitamin D supplementation to prevent deficiencies.
Symptom improvement in FPIAP often occurs within 48–72 hours of dietary elimination.
Extensively hydrolyzed formula (eHF) is recommended as the initial substitute formula, with escalation to amino acid formula (AAF) for severe cases or non-response after 14 days.
Soy formula is only recommended after 6 months of age due to cross-allergy risks, especially in infants with symptoms starting before 6 months.
Unmodified goat, buffalo, or sheep milk and diluted milk or rice-water feeding are unsafe and can lead to growth faltering and nutrient deficiencies.
reference_quote: Indian guidance recommends starting with extensively hydrolyzed formula (eHF) for most infants; escalate to amino acid formula (AAF) for severe disease or lack of response to eHF after an adequately strict trial (about 14 days).
